name: Ojos del Cabriel and Molino de San Pedro Waterfall Trail
name_original: Las Cascadas del Vallecillo — Serranía de Cuenca
description: Hike the moderate 4.6-mile Ojos del Cabriel and Molino de San Pedro Waterfall Trail to discover turquoise waters and stunning cascades.
region_intro: Hiking trails around El Vallecillo are situated in the Sierra de Albarracín, Teruel, Spain, at altitudes between 1400 and 1600 meters. The region is characterized by extensive mixed pine forests, interspersed with juniper and holm oak, and features a landscape rich in karst formations. Notable natural features include the source of the Cabriel River, known as the Ojos del Cabriel, and the Cascada de la Herrería waterfall. The terrain transitions from a Cretaceous limestone ridge towards the Serranía de Cuenca. Best hiking trails around El Vallecillo The most popular hiking route is Las Cascadas del Vallecillo — Serranía de Cuenca, a 4.6 miles (7.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 7 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores the area's waterfalls and natural beauty. Another top favourite among local hikers is Toril - El Vallecillo through the Eyes of the Cabriel and the San Pedro Mill Waterfall (GR 10.1), a difficult 12.2 miles (19.7 km) path. This challenging trail connects key natural landmarks, including the source of the Cabriel River and the San Pedro Mill Waterfall. Local hikers also love the Ojos del Cabriel – Herrería Waterfall loop from El Vallecillo, a 4.1 miles (6.6 km) trail leading through the area's significant water features, often completed in about 1 hour 53 minutes. Hiking around El Vallecillo is defined by pine forests, river valleys, and karst formations, offering a network of routes for various ability levels. The routes in El Vallecillo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.9 stars from over 25 reviews. More than 195 hikers have used komoot to explore El Vallecillo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when hiking in El Vallecillo?
A: El Vallecillo, situated in the Sierra de Albarracín, offers diverse terrain characterized by extensive mixed pine forests, interspersed with juniper and holm oak. You'll encounter a landscape rich in karst formations like dolinas and simas, as well as a Cretaceous limestone ridge. The region also features river valleys, particularly along the Cabriel River, providing varied walking surfaces.

Q: Are there many hiking trails available in El Vallecillo?
A: Yes, El Vallecillo boasts nearly 20 hiking routes, catering to various skill levels. You'll find a good mix, with 5 easy trails, 10 moderate options, and 3 more challenging routes for experienced hikers.

Q: Can I find trails that feature waterfalls in El Vallecillo?
A: Absolutely. The region is famous for its waterfalls. The impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5247809" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Herrería Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; (also known as the 'Hidden Waterfall' or Cascada del Molino de San Pedro) is a highlight. You can explore it on routes like the easy &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10374295" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos del Cabriel – Herrería Waterfall loop from El Vallecillo&lt;/a&gt;, which also visits the source of the Cabriel River.

Q: Where can I see the source of the Cabriel River?
A: The natural springs that mark the official source of the Cabriel River, known as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4547251" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos del Cabriel&lt;/a&gt;, are a significant natural feature in the area. These 'eyes' are where groundwater emerges from Cretaceous limestone. An excellent way to visit them is via the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10374295" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos del Cabriel – Herrería Waterfall loop from El Vallecillo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in El Vallecillo?
A: Yes, there are several easy routes suitable for families. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10374295" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos del Cabriel – Herrería Waterfall loop from El Vallecillo&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 4.1-mile (6.6 km) trail that takes about 1 hour 53 minutes, perfect for a family outing to see the waterfalls and river source.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in El Vallecillo?
A: El Vallecillo, situated at 1400 to 1600 meters altitude, offers pleasant hiking conditions during spring, summer, and autumn. Spring brings lush greenery and flowing waterfalls, while summer provides warm, sunny days ideal for exploring the pine forests. Autumn showcases beautiful fall colors. Winters can be cold, with potential for snow, which might appeal to those seeking a different kind of outdoor experience.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in the area?
A: Yes, many trails in El Vallecillo are designed as loops. A popular circular route is the easy &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10374295" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos del Cabriel – Herrería Waterfall loop from El Vallecillo&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 4.1 miles (6.6 km) and allows you to experience key water features without retracing your steps.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in El Vallecillo?
A: The hiking routes in El Vallecillo are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average score of 3.9 stars from over 25 reviews. Hikers often praise the natural beauty, particularly the waterfalls and the source of the Cabriel River, as well as the well-maintained paths through the pine forests.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: For those seeking a more demanding adventure, El Vallecillo offers difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e976187634/toril-el-vallecillo-through-the-eyes-of-the-cabriel-and-the-san-pedro-mill-waterfall-gr-10-1" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Toril - El Vallecillo through the Eyes of the Cabriel and the San Pedro Mill Waterfall (GR 10.1)&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 12.2-mile (19.7 km) path that connects significant natural landmarks, providing a strenuous but rewarding experience.

Q: What natural landmarks or points of interest can I explore besides waterfalls?
A: Beyond the famous waterfalls, you can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4547251" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos del Cabriel&lt;/a&gt;, the natural springs forming the source of the Cabriel River. The region is also known for its karst formations and is home to the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4547205" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monumental Pine&lt;/a&gt;. For those interested in geology, the Muela de Javalón is a prominent 'colgado' synclinal formation.

Q: Is parking available near the trailheads in El Vallecillo?
A: While specific parking details for every trailhead are not provided, El Vallecillo is a small village in a rural area. Generally, you can find parking in or near the village center, which serves as a common starting point for many local trails, including those leading to the waterfalls and the Ojos del Cabriel.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the hiking trails in El Vallecillo?
A: El Vallecillo is a natural area, and generally, dogs are welcome on trails, especially if kept on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure the comfort of other hikers. Always ensure you clean up after your pet to maintain the pristine environment of the Sierra de Albarracín.
tips: Without a doubt one of the most beautiful waterfalls with a pool I have been to in Spain. Its location, in what is called "emptied Spain" due to its low population density, has prevented its overcrowding, although in summer there were quite a few people bathing. It is advisable to wear swimming or canyoning shoes to enter and exit the water through the porous stones. The view from the cavity behind the waterfall is simply stunning.

The Cascada de la Herrería is a natural gem located in the Sierra de Albarracín, near the town of El Vallecillo. This beautiful waterfall, also known as the “Hidden Waterfall,” has a drop of approximately 8 meters over the Cabriel River. 
To reach the waterfall, you can follow a hiking route that begins in El Vallecillo. The route is short, just half a kilometer, and takes you through a varied and picturesque landscape. During the walk, you will also be able to see the natural springs known as the Ojos del Cabriel, which feed the river and the waterfall.
The route can be done along the track, at a certain height, or right next to the river on a path. If the river is not in flood, it is advisable to walk along the path next to the river.

The water springs that supply the river, in this case the Cabriel, are known as Ojos. The water springs from the ground and in this area there are two of them very close. It is reached by a very entertaining path without technical difficulty.

faqs.question: How long does it take to hike the Ojos del Cabriel and Molino de San Pedro Waterfall Trail?
faqs.answer: The Komoot route for this trail is approximately 7.5 km (4.7 miles) long and typically takes around 2 hours and 7 minutes to complete. This duration is for the main loop, but you might want to allow extra time to enjoy the waterfalls and viewpoints.

faqs.question: Is the Ojos del Cabriel and Molino de San Pedro Waterfall Trail suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally considered easy to moderate, making it suitable for most skill levels, including families with children. The paths are largely accessible, with minimal elevation gain for the shorter loop options. However, some sections, especially behind the San Pedro Mill Waterfall, can be slippery and require sure-footedness.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail is rich in natural beauty and historical sites. Key highlights include the powerful springs of Ojos del Cabriel, the stunning San Pedro Mill Waterfall (where you can walk behind the cascade), and the 'Hidden Waterfall' or Herrería Waterfall. You'll also follow a beautiful Riverside trail, pass through pine forests, and might spot the ancient Torre Cavero defensive tower.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to visit the Ojos del Cabriel and Molino de San Pedro Waterfall Trail?
faqs.answer: For the most impressive water flow at the waterfalls, winter or spring are ideal. If you prefer milder weather and vibrant natural colors, spring and autumn are excellent choices. While beautiful, summer can attract more crowds, and the water levels might be lower.

faqs.question: Is the trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Ojos del Cabriel and Molino de San Pedro Waterfall Trail is considered dog-friendly. Many hikers enjoy bringing their canine companions along. Please ensure your dog is kept on a leash and that you clean up after them to preserve the natural environment for everyone.

faqs.question: Where can I park for the trailhead?
faqs.answer: Specific parking information is not detailed in the provided sources, but trails of this nature typically have designated parking areas near the main access points, often close to the village of El Vallecillo in Teruel. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The sources do not mention any specific permits or entrance fees for hiking the Ojos del Cabriel and Molino de San Pedro Waterfall Trail itself. As it's located in the Sierra de Albarracín, it's generally accessible to the public. However, always respect local regulations and signage.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail traverses a landscape rich in water, following the Cabriel River. You'll encounter a sinuous karst canyon with rapids, backwaters, and pools. The paths are generally comfortable, winding through dense foliage and pine forests. Some sections, particularly near the waterfalls, can be damp or slippery.

faqs.question: Can I swim in the pools at the waterfalls?
faqs.answer: The deep, crystal-clear, turquoise pool at the base of the San Pedro Mill Waterfall is a popular spot for bathing. Be aware that the water can be quite chilly, even in warmer months. Always exercise caution when entering natural waters.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with any other hiking routes?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route is part of the GR 10.1, a European long-distance path. Additionally, sections of the trail overlap with other local paths, including the "Rubiales - Orihuela del Tremedal" (covering about 37% of the route), "Camino del molino de La Herrería" (29%), and "Camino de los Ojos del Cabriel" (10%).

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or restrooms near the trailhead?
faqs.answer: The area, sometimes referred to as 'emptied Spain,' is less densely populated, which contributes to its quiet atmosphere. While specific amenities are not detailed, it's generally a good idea to bring your own water and snacks. You might find facilities in the nearby village of El Vallecillo.

faqs.question: What is the 'insider tip' for hiking this trail?
faqs.answer: For a truly unique experience, don't miss the opportunity to walk behind the San Pedro Mill Waterfall. It offers a stunning and memorable perspective. However, be aware that this section can be quite slippery and potentially dangerous, so proceed with caution and appropriate footwear.
